description_long: This object consists of three irregularly shaped stone blocks, each with faceted surfaces and a muted gray coloration with subtle greenish undertones near the base. The largest block features a shallow groove or crack on its upper surface, adding a touch of naturalistic detail. The blocks have a polygonal, roughly hewn appearance, suggesting they were shaped by hand or weathered over time. Their surfaces are matte and slightly rough, with no visible polish or gloss. The blocks are grouped closely together, with the two smaller stones positioned adjacent to the larger one, creating a clustered arrangement. The overall look is utilitarian and robust, suitable for use as a barrier or obstacle in an urban or landscaped environment.
description: A cluster of three faceted, gray stone blocks with rough, matte surfaces and subtle greenish bases, arranged closely together. The largest block features a shallow groove on top, giving a natural, weathered appearance.
description_view_0: View 0 shows the largest block in the foreground with its angled top and visible groove, while the two smaller blocks are positioned to the right.
description_view_1: View 1 presents the blocks from the opposite side, highlighting the flat face of the largest block and the relative heights of the two smaller blocks.
description_view_2: View 2 offers a diagonal perspective, emphasizing the depth and polygonal facets of the largest block, with the smaller blocks receding into the background.
description_view_3: View 3 displays the rear of the largest block, showing its broad, flat surface and the side of one smaller block partially visible.
attribute_location_context: urban sidewalk or landscaped park barrier
attribute_installation_method: dry-stacked or placed directly on the ground
attribute_weathering_pattern: Slight discoloration and subtle greenish tint near the base suggest mild weathering or moss growth; otherwise, surfaces appear intact with minor surface roughness.
attribute_integration_with_surrounding_s: clustered together as an isolated group
